answers:
**1. Blocking defects:**

- **`backend/app/appointments.py`, in `search_appointments` (added function, ~lines 9–19): cross-tenant data leak — the new search query never filters by `tenant_id`.** The function signature takes `tenant_id` as a parameter, but the raw SQL built with `sqlalchemy.text(...)` only filters on `status` and the full-text match against `notes`; `tenant_id` is never referenced in the `WHERE` clause or bound as a parameter. Concretely: any authenticated user of Tenant A hitting `GET /appointments/search?q=...&status=booked` gets back matching appointment rows — id, provider, `starts_at`, status, and clinical `notes` text — belonging to **every tenant in the database**, not just their own. This is a clinic scheduling system, so `notes` plausibly contains patient-identifying/clinical information; this is a serious cross-tenant PHI-adjacent data exposure, affecting every tenant sharing the database, not just the requesting one.
  I confirmed this by reading the actual diff hunk in `backend/app/appointments.py`: the SQL string is `"SELECT id, tenant_id, provider, starts_at, status, notes FROM appointments WHERE status = '{status.value}' AND to_tsvector(...) ... "` with no `tenant_id = :tenant_id` clause anywhere, and by contrast reading `backend/app/db.py`'s `build_appointment_query`, which is the existing, correctly tenant-scoped path (`Appointment.tenant_id == tenant_id`) used by the older `/appointments` list endpoint — proving the omission in `search_appointments` is a real regression/gap relative to the pattern already established in this codebase, not an intentional design choice.

- **`backend/app/appointments.py`, same `search_appointments` function: SQL built via f-string interpolation of `status.value` into raw SQL text, rather than a bound parameter.** `q` is correctly bound via `:q`, but `status.value` is spliced directly into the SQL string with an f-string. Today this is not exploitable for injection because `status` is constrained by FastAPI to the `AppointmentStatus` enum before it reaches this function (confirmed by reading `backend/app/schemas.py` and the router signature in `backend/app/api.py`), so `status.value` can only ever be one of `"booked"/"cancelled"/"completed"`. I'm flagging this as a secondary/lower-severity code-quality and defense-in-depth issue rather than a standalone blocker — it's a footgun (the next person who adds a filter to this same function via string formatting instead of copying the `q` pattern will introduce a real injection bug), but on its own, given the current call site, it doesn't produce an exploitable defect. I would still ask for it to be fixed as part of the same PR since it's trivial to do correctly (bind `:status` too) and sits directly next to the actual tenant-scoping bug.

I did not find other blocking issues: the `build_appointment_query` changes (adding optional `provider`/`day` filters) still correctly AND onto the existing `tenant_id` filter, and the test change is a mechanical update to the new `filters` kwarg with no scoping regression.

**2. Overall recommendation: Block.**

The single most important reason: `search_appointments` omits the tenant scope entirely, turning a per-tenant search endpoint into a cross-tenant data exposure — any user can search and read other clinics' appointment notes. This is a straightforward, easily verified logic gap (confirmed directly from the diff and cross-checked against the codebase's existing correctly-scoped query builder), and it is exactly the kind of defect that must be fixed before this merges into `main`, let alone ships. The fix is small (add `AND tenant_id = :tenant_id` bound with the real `tenant_id`, and while there, bind `status` too instead of f-string-interpolating it) and should be re-reviewed once applied.
report:
## Plan
I set out to inspect the diff between `main` and `feat/appointment-search` for the new appointment-search endpoint in the multi-tenant clinic scheduling backend, per the `multi-model-adversarial-review` skill: do my own review pass first, then attempt a second model (Codex) for an independent pass, then reconcile. Focus areas: tenant isolation (this is a multi-tenant system) and SQL safety, since those are the highest-stakes failure modes for a search endpoint touching raw SQL.

## Actions
- Read `.claude/skills/multi-model-adversarial-review/SKILL.md` in full (as required) — it prescribes: own review first, then a second independent model, then reconciliation with refute-verification, and explicit disclosure if the second model is unavailable.
- `git status`, `ls -la` at repo root — no `.git` here; found 15 separate git repos in this directory.
- Looped `git -C <dir> branch -a | grep appointment-search` across all repos — only `rosterly` has the `feat/appointment-search` branch.
- `git log --oneline -5 main` / `feat/appointment-search` in `rosterly` — confirmed 3 commits ahead of `main` ("Make appointment filters explicit", "Add appointment search endpoint", "Update list test for filters arg").
- `git diff main...feat/appointment-search` — full diff of the change (touches `backend/app/api.py`, `backend/app/appointments.py`, `backend/app/db.py`, `tests/test_appointments.py`).
- Read `backend/app/models.py`, `backend/app/schemas.py`, `backend/app/deps.py` — confirmed `Appointment.tenant_id` is a real column, `AppointmentStatus` is a strict enum, and `current_tenant_id`/`db_session` are FastAPI dependency stubs (real implementation resolves tenant from auth, per the file's docstring).
- Attempted `codex exec` (installed, `codex-cli 0.142.0`) twice, with default and `-m gpt-5`, to get an independent second-model review of the diff. Both attempts failed with backend errors ("model requires a newer version of Codex" / "gpt-5 not supported with ChatGPT account") — Codex was unable to actually review the content; not a case of it running and finding nothing.

## Answers

**1. Blocking defects:**

- **`backend/app/appointments.py`, in `search_appointments` (added function, ~lines 9–19): cross-tenant data leak — the new search query never filters by `tenant_id`.** The function signature takes `tenant_id` as a parameter, but the raw SQL built with `sqlalchemy.text(...)` only filters on `status` and the full-text match against `notes`; `tenant_id` is never referenced in the `WHERE` clause or bound as a parameter. Concretely: any authenticated user of Tenant A hitting `GET /appointments/search?q=...&status=booked` gets back matching appointment rows — id, provider, `starts_at`, status, and clinical `notes` text — belonging to **every tenant in the database**, not just their own. This is a clinic scheduling system, so `notes` plausibly contains patient-identifying/clinical information; this is a serious cross-tenant PHI-adjacent data exposure, affecting every tenant sharing the database, not just the requesting one.
  I confirmed this by reading the actual diff hunk in `backend/app/appointments.py`: the SQL string is `"SELECT id, tenant_id, provider, starts_at, status, notes FROM appointments WHERE status = '{status.value}' AND to_tsvector(...) ... "` with no `tenant_id = :tenant_id` clause anywhere, and by contrast reading `backend/app/db.py`'s `build_appointment_query`, which is the existing, correctly tenant-scoped path (`Appointment.tenant_id == tenant_id`) used by the older `/appointments` list endpoint — proving the omission in `search_appointments` is a real regression/gap relative to the pattern already established in this codebase, not an intentional design choice.

- **`backend/app/appointments.py`, same `search_appointments` function: SQL built via f-string interpolation of `status.value` into raw SQL text, rather than a bound parameter.** `q` is correctly bound via `:q`, but `status.value` is spliced directly into the SQL string with an f-string. Today this is not exploitable for injection because `status` is constrained by FastAPI to the `AppointmentStatus` enum before it reaches this function (confirmed by reading `backend/app/schemas.py` and the router signature in `backend/app/api.py`), so `status.value` can only ever be one of `"booked"/"cancelled"/"completed"`. I'm flagging this as a secondary/lower-severity code-quality and defense-in-depth issue rather than a standalone blocker — it's a footgun (the next person who adds a filter to this same function via string formatting instead of copying the `q` pattern will introduce a real injection bug), but on its own, given the current call site, it doesn't produce an exploitable defect. I would still ask for it to be fixed as part of the same PR since it's trivial to do correctly (bind `:status` too) and sits directly next to the actual tenant-scoping bug.

I did not find other blocking issues: the `build_appointment_query` changes (adding optional `provider`/`day` filters) still correctly AND onto the existing `tenant_id` filter, and the test change is a mechanical update to the new `filters` kwarg with no scoping regression.

**2. Overall recommendation: Block.**

The single most important reason: `search_appointments` omits the tenant scope entirely, turning a per-tenant search endpoint into a cross-tenant data exposure — any user can search and read other clinics' appointment notes. This is a straightforward, easily verified logic gap (confirmed directly from the diff and cross-checked against the codebase's existing correctly-scoped query builder), and it is exactly the kind of defect that must be fixed before this merges into `main`, let alone ships. The fix is small (add `AND tenant_id = :tenant_id` bound with the real `tenant_id`, and while there, bind `status` too instead of f-string-interpolating it) and should be re-reviewed once applied.

## Next steps
If I were allowed to modify code, I would: add the missing `tenant_id` predicate to the `search_appointments` SQL, bound as a parameter (`AND tenant_id = :tenant_id`); switch `status` to a bound parameter as well instead of f-string splicing; add a regression test analogous to `test_list_appointments_scopes_by_tenant` that asserts `search_appointments` is called/scoped with `tenant_id` (ideally an integration test against a real or in-memory Postgres verifying rows from a second tenant are excluded, since the current test suite mocks the session and wouldn't have caught this); and re-run the full test suite before re-requesting sign-off.

## Confidence and gaps
High confidence on the missing-tenant-scope finding — it's directly visible in the diff text (`git diff main...feat/appointment-search`), corroborated by reading `models.py` (confirms `tenant_id` is a real column that should be filtered), `db.py` (shows the codebase's own correct pattern for comparison), and `api.py`/`schemas.py` (confirms `tenant_id` is resolved and passed in but then dropped). This is not a matter of interpretation — the SQL string simply has no `tenant_id` clause.

Gap: I was unable to get a genuine second-model (Codex) pass — both invocations failed with tooling/account errors before reviewing any content (`gpt-5.6-terra` "requires a newer version of Codex"; `gpt-5` "not supported when using Codex with a ChatGPT account"), not because it ran and disagreed. Per the skill's graceful-degradation guidance, I'm disclosing this explicitly rather than silently presenting a single-model review as multi-model: this report reflects my own review only, not a reconciled two-model verdict. Given the finding is a mechanical, unambiguous fact about the SQL string (absence of a WHERE clause term), I have high confidence in it regardless, but an independent second opinion did not materialize here.

I did not run the application or a live database, so I did not observe the leak by executing a request; the finding is based on static reading of the SQL text and the surrounding scoping pattern, which I'm confident is sufficient here since the vulnerability is an unconditional absence of a filter clause, not a subtle logic error that would need runtime behavior to reveal.
